title: Energy-I-Score: Implementation Details
text: vignette presents implementation details energy--Score, metric designed evaluate quality imputation methods incomplete datasets. score based concept energy distance observed imputed distributions. allows comparing uncertainty induced imputation model variability present observed data. procedure model-agnostic: can used imputation method ℐ\mathcal{} multiple imputation draws. score distribution-free can applied : continuous variables, mixed-type data (score calculated dummy variables), multiple imputation methods.

title: Energy-I-Score: Implementation Details
text: Let X∈ℝn×pX \\mathbb{R}^{n \times p} original dataset missing values, X̃∈ℝn×p\tilde{X} \\mathbb{R}^{n \times p} imputed dataset, ℐ\mathcal{} imputation function, NN number imputations drawn ℐ\mathcal{}. , variable missing values j∈{1,…,p}j \\{1, \ldots, p\} define LjL_j set indices ii Xi,jX_{,j} observed, LjcL_j^c set indices ii Xi,jX_{,j} missing OjO_j set fully observed predictor variables rows Xi,jX_{,j} observed. Finally, define set variables missing values 𝒮={j:Ljc≠∅}\mathcal{S} = \{ j : L_j^c \neq \emptyset \}.

title: Energy-I-Score: Implementation Details
text: energy--Score computed iteratively variable missing data. following steps performed j∈𝒮j \\mathcal{S}.

title: Energy-I-Score: Implementation Details
text: determine set predictor variables: Oj=⋂m∈Lj{l:ml=0}. O_j = \bigcap_{m \L_j} \{ l : m_l = 0 \}. OjO_j empty, algorithm automatically selects fallback variable k*k^* defined : k*=argmaxk≠j|{:mi,⋅∈Lj∩Lk}|. k^* = \text{argmax}_{k \neq j} \big|\{ : m_{,\cdot} \L_j \cap L_k \}\big|.  variable largest number observed values observed part column jj. ensures imputation model least one predictor.

title: Energy-I-Score: Implementation Details
text: data split training test sets follows: Train=[NAX̃Lj,OjX̃Ljc,jX̃Ljc,Oj],Test=[X̃Lj,j]. \text{Train} = \begin{bmatrix} \mathrm{NA} & \tilde{X}_{L_j, O_j} \\ \tilde{X}_{L_j^c, j} & \tilde{X}_{L_j^c, O_j} \end{bmatrix}, \quad \text{Test} = \begin{bmatrix} \tilde{X}_{L_j, j} \end{bmatrix}. training set contains observed predictor values missing target values imputed. test set contains observed target values evaluate imputation quality.

title: Energy-I-Score: Implementation Details
text: missing part training set imputed NN times using ℐ\mathcal{}: X̃,j(1),…,X̃,j(N)∼HXj|XOj,Mj=1. \tilde{X}_{,j}^{(1)}, \ldots, \tilde{X}_{,j}^{(N)} \sim H_{X_j|X_{O_j}, M_j = 1}. imputation represents draw conditional distribution missing variable given observed predictors.

title: Energy-I-Score: Implementation Details
text: ∈Lji \L_j, energy--Score component computed : ŜNAj(H,P)=1|Lj|∑∈Lj[12N2∑l=1N∑ℓ=1N|X̃,j(l)−X̃,j(ℓ)|−1N∑l=1N|X̃,j(l)−xi,j|]. \widehat{S}^j_{\mathrm{NA}}(H,P) =  \frac{1}{|L_j|} \sum_{\L_j}  \left[ \frac{1}{2N^2} \sum_{l=1}^N \sum_{\ell=1}^N  |\tilde{X}_{,j}^{(l)} - \tilde{X}_{,j}^{(\ell)}| -  \frac{1}{N} \sum_{l=1}^N  |\tilde{X}_{,j}^{(l)} - x_{,j}| \right]. first term internal dispersion imputed values second term distance imputed actual observations. larger score, greater uncertainty imputation relative true data.

title: Energy-I-Score: Implementation Details
text: variable’s contribution final score weighted : wj=1n2|Lj|⋅|Ljc|. w_j = \frac{1}{n^2} |L_j| \cdot |L_j^c|. accounts relative amount missing observed data per variable.

title: Energy-I-Score: Implementation Details
text: final energy--Score weighted average variables missing values: ŜNA(H,P)=1|𝒮|∑j∈𝒮wjŜNAj(H,P). \widehat{S}_{\mathrm{NA}}(H,P) = \frac{1}{|\mathcal{S}|} \sum_{j \\mathcal{S}}  w_j \widehat{S}^j_{\mathrm{NA}}(H,P). scalar measure summarizes imputation uncertainty across dataset.

title: Energy-I-Score: Implementation Details
text: High values score suggest large variability poor alignment imputed observed distributions. Low values indicate imputations close observed data distribution (better performance). Variables missing values lower weight, many missing values contribute . Methods rely multiple imputation weak/random draw mechanism tend perform worse, underestimate uncertainty missing values. energy--Score primarily used rank different imputation methods, rather interpret absolute numeric value directly.

title: Energy-I-Score: Implementation Details
text: approach follows methodology proposed Näf, Grzesiak, Scornet (2025) “rank imputation methods?” (arXiv:2507.11297).

title: Energy-I-Score: First Steps
text: Iscores package provides tools evaluating imputation methods using Imputation Scores (IScores). particular, package implements DR -Score energy--Score, measure quality imputed datasets comparing relationships observed imputed values. methodology described detail Näf et al. (2022) Näf et al. (2025). package supports: numerical datasets, mixed datasets containing numerical categorical variables, evaluation single imputation method, comparison multiple imputation methods. main functions : energy_IScore() — calculates Energy--Score single imputation method, compare_Iscores() — compares several imputation methods using selected IScores. vignette presents basic workflow computing Imputation Scores comparing imputation approaches.

title: Energy-I-Score: First Steps
text: stable version package can installed CRAN (soon): development version can installed GitHub: installation, load package set random seed ensure reproducibility:
code: install.packages("Iscores") if (!requireNamespace("devtools", quietly = TRUE)) {   install.packages("devtools") } devtools::install_github("missValTeam/Iscores") library(Iscores)

title: Energy-I-Score: First Steps
text: package expects data.frame containing missing values represented NA. Numerical variables stored numeric vectors. Categorical variables stored factors intended treated categorical score calculation. demonstration purposes, use randomly generated data MCAR missings:

title: Energy-I-Score: First Steps
text: computing Imputation Score, first need define imputation method applied incomplete dataset. Iscores package flexible allows user evaluate imputation approach, provided imputation function satisfies simple requirements. imputation function: Must accept dataset missing values first argument. Must return completed dataset dimensions input data. return dataset without missing values. Can represent: simple custom imputation strategy, wrapper around external function, package programming language. example, let’s define zero imputation : function can now passed directly energy_IScore(), DR_IScore(), compare_Iscores().
code: impute_zero <- function(X) {       X[is.na(X)] <- 0      return(X)  }

title: Energy-I-Score: First Steps
text: imputation function defined, can evaluate using energy_IScore(). example , calculate energy--Score impute_zero() method. result single weighted score summarizing imputation performance across variables containing missing values. addition, detailed information variable returned attribute result. table contains: variable-specific scores, aggregation weights, number variables used training. table can accessed using attr(): Note weighted score simply weighted mean scores particular variables:
code: sc <- energy_IScore(X = X, imputation_func = impute_zero)  sc #> [1] 0.799712 #> attr(,"dat") #>    column_id weight     score n_columns_used #> X1         1 0.1771 0.7738732              1 #> X3         3 0.1771 0.7737987              1 #> X2         2 0.1539 0.7941482              1 #> X4         4 0.1411 0.8707366              1 attr(sc, "dat") #>    column_id weight     score n_columns_used #> X1         1 0.1771 0.7738732              1 #> X3         3 0.1771 0.7737987              1 #> X2         2 0.1539 0.7941482              1 #> X4         4 0.1411 0.8707366              1 sum(attr(sc, "dat")[["score"]] * attr(sc, "dat")[["weight"]]) / sum(attr(sc, "dat")[["weight"]]) #> [1] 0.799712

title: Energy-I-Score: First Steps
text: energy_IScore() function exposes several parameters control scoring procedure.

title: Energy-I-Score: First Steps
text: parameter N controls many times missing part re-imputed score estimation. parameter mainly relevant stochastic multiple imputation methods. deterministic methods, setting multiple = FALSE automatically forces N = 1. Note hen N = 1, imputation method always returns completed dataset, predictive distribution effectively represented single point estimate. cases, energy--Score computed degenerate empirical distribution, may provide less reliable approximation uncertainty. Consequently, score naturally favors imputation methods generate realistic variability sample well conditional distribution missing values, rather methods always return fixed imputations.
code: energy_IScore(X = X, imputation_func = impute_zero, N = 5) #> [1] 0.799712 #> attr(,"dat") #>    column_id weight     score n_columns_used #> X1         1 0.1771 0.7738732              1 #> X3         3 0.1771 0.7737987              1 #> X2         2 0.1539 0.7941482              1 #> X4         4 0.1411 0.8707366              1

what: Limiting the number of scored variables: max_length
title: Energy-I-Score: First Steps
text: datasets many incomplete variables, score computation may become time-consuming. max_length argument allows user limit number variables used score calculation. Variables largest number missing values selected first. default, max_length = NULL, meaning incomplete variables included.
code: energy_IScore(X = X, imputation_func = impute_zero, max_length = 2) #> [1] 0.773836 #> attr(,"dat") #>    column_id weight     score n_columns_used #> X1         1 0.1771 0.7738732              1 #> X3         3 0.1771 0.7737987              1

title: Energy-I-Score: First Steps
text: variables may enough fully observed predictors available training. situations: skip_if_needed = TRUE (default) removes minimal number observations construct valid training set, skip_if_needed = FALSE returns NA variables complete predictors can identified.

title: Energy-I-Score: First Steps
text: Setting scale = TRUE standardizes variables internally score calculation. can useful variables different numerical ranges, preventing large-scale variables dominating score.

title: Energy-I-Score: First Steps
text: mentioned earlier, categorical variables must stored factors order handled correctly energy_IScore(). input data contains least one factor variable, energy_IScore() automatically switches mixed-data version score. Therefore, users need call separate function mixed datasets. construct simple mixed dataset containing numerical categorical variables. Missing values generated according MCAR mechanism. use simple median/mode imputation function. Numerical variables imputed median, factor variables imputed frequent category. score can calculated public function numerical data: Internally, categorical variables transformed using one-hot encoding score computed multivariate energy distances. additional implementation details methodological discussion, see vignette “Energy--Score: Implementation Details”.
code: set.seed(10)  X_cat <- Iscores:::random_mcar_mixed_data(100, 4)  head(X_cat) #>          col1       col2      col3       col4 col5 #> 1  0.01874617 -0.7618043        NA         NA    1 #> 2 -0.18425254         NA 0.3308765  0.5904095    3 #> 3 -1.37133055 -1.0399434 1.3902751 -0.6306855 <NA> #> 4 -0.59916772  0.7115740 0.8720470  0.7923495    4 #> 5  0.29454513         NA        NA  0.1253846 <NA> #> 6  0.38979430  0.5631747 0.4958216         NA    3 impute_mean_mode <- Iscores:::median_mode_imputation energy_IScore(X = X_cat, imputation_func = impute_mean_mode) #> Factor variables detected. #>              Calculating the energy-I-Score for mixed data. #> [1] 0.8155521 #> attr(,"dat") #>      column_id weight     score n_columns_used #> col3         3 0.1924 0.7758805              1 #> col1         1 0.1771 0.7280336              1 #> col5         5 0.1539 0.9428090              1 #> col2         2 0.1411 0.8319434              1 #> col4         4 0.1411 0.8243028              1

title: Energy-I-Score: First Steps
text: package also provides DR_IScore() function based density-ratio estimation random projection forests. Unlike energy_IScore(), evaluates predictive distributions scoring rules, DR_IScore() compares distributions observed imputed data using projected random forests density-ratio estimation. parameter m controls number imputed datasets generated imputation method. Increasing m may improve score stability stochastic imputation procedures.
code: sc_dr <- DR_IScore(X = X,                    imputation_func = impute_zero,                    m = 3,                    n_proj = 10,                    n_trees_per_proj = 2,                    n_cores = 1)  sc_dr #> [1] -8.327919

title: Energy-I-Score: First Steps
text: parameters n_proj n_trees_per_proj control complexity random projection forests used internally: larger n_proj increases number random projections considered, larger n_trees_per_proj increases number trees grown projection. Increasing parameters may improve stability precision score, also increases computational cost.

title: Energy-I-Score: First Steps
text: compare_Iscores() function can used compare several imputation methods simultaneously. define two additional imputation strategies mice package. Now place methods named list: can now compare methods using energy--Score: resulting data frame contains one row per imputation method.
code: library(mice)   impute_mice_norm <- function(X) {   imp <- mice(X, m = 1, method = "norm", maxit = 5, printFlag = FALSE)      complete(imp) }  impute_mice_rf <- function(X) {   imp <- mice(X, m = 1, method = "rf", maxit = 5, printFlag = FALSE)      complete(imp) } methods_list <- list(zero = impute_zero,                      mice_norm = impute_mice_norm,                      mice_rf = impute_mice_rf) sc_comparison <- compare_Iscores(X = X,                                  methods_list = methods_list,                                  score = "energy_IScore",                                  N = 10,                                  silent = TRUE) #> Calculating the energy_IScore for method zero ... #> Calculating the energy_IScore for method mice_norm ... #> Calculating the energy_IScore for method mice_rf ...  sc_comparison #>       score    score_name    method #> 1 0.7997120 energy_IScore      zero #> 2 0.6247271 energy_IScore mice_norm #> 3 0.6839281 energy_IScore   mice_rf

title: Energy-I-Score: First Steps
text: package also allows simultaneous comparison using multiple scoring rules. multiple scores requested, additional arguments passed compare_Iscores() automatically forwarded corresponding scoring functions. example : N silent arguments used energy_IScore(), m, n_proj, n_trees_per_proj arguments used DR_IScore(). Therefore, combining several scoring rules, users provide parameters required selected score.
code: comparison_all <- compare_Iscores(X = X,                                   methods_list = methods_list,                                   score = c("energy_IScore", "DR_IScore"),                                   N = 10,                                   m = 3,                                   n_proj = 10,                                   n_trees_per_proj = 2,                                   silent = TRUE) #> Calculating the energy_IScore for method zero ... #> Calculating the energy_IScore for method mice_norm ... #> Calculating the energy_IScore for method mice_rf ... #> Calculating the DR_IScore for method zero ... #> Calculating the DR_IScore for method mice_norm ... #> Calculating the DR_IScore for method mice_rf ...  comparison_all #>        score    score_name    method #> 1  0.7997120 energy_IScore      zero #> 2  0.6473443 energy_IScore mice_norm #> 3  0.6753519 energy_IScore   mice_rf #> 4 -9.2208219     DR_IScore      zero #> 5  1.4280980     DR_IScore mice_norm #> 6  2.4883888     DR_IScore   mice_rf

title: Energy-I-Score: First Steps
text: Use multiple = TRUE genuinely multiple imputers determine N stable estimates. Consider scale = TRUE mixing variables different scales. Use max_length quick experiments; remove final runs. Keep skip_if_needed = TRUE unless explicitly want flag unscorable columns NA.

title: Energy-I-Score: First Steps
text: access original dataset imputation, can also use energy distance additional evaluation metric. package provides easy--use wrapper edistance() around energy::eqdist.e function energy package.
code: X_observed <- matrix(rnorm(2000), ncol = 4)    X_miss <- X_observed X_miss[runif(nrow(X_miss) * ncol(X_miss)) < 0.2] <- NA  edistance(X_observed, impute_zero(X_miss)) #> E-statistic  #>    4.360287  edistance(X_observed, impute_mice_norm(X_miss)) #> E-statistic  #>   0.5447785

what: Proper Scoring Rules for Missing Value Imputation
title: Proper Scoring Rules for Missing Value Imputation
text: Iscores provides scoring rules evaluating comparing imputation methods. package implements methodology introduced Näf et al. (2022) Näf, Grzesiak, Scornet (2025). package supports: numerical datasets, mixed numerical categorical data, deterministic imputations, stochastic multiple imputations, comparison several imputation methods.

title: Proper Scoring Rules for Missing Value Imputation
code: install.packages("Iscores")

title: Proper Scoring Rules for Missing Value Imputation
code: install.packages("devtools")  devtools::install_github("missValTeam/Iscores")

title: Proper Scoring Rules for Missing Value Imputation
text: package evaluates user-defined imputation methods. imputation function must: accept dataset missing values, return completed dataset dimensions. define simple zero-imputation method. now generate example data missing values.
code: library(Iscores)  impute_zero <- function(X) {    X[is.na(X)] <- 0    X } set.seed(10)  X <- Iscores:::random_mcar_data(100, 4)  head(X)

title: Proper Scoring Rules for Missing Value Imputation
text: energy_IScore() function evaluates quality imputation method. Detailed variable-level results stored attribute:
code: sc <- energy_IScore(   X = X,   imputation_func = impute_zero,   N = 10,   silent = TRUE )  sc attr(sc, "dat")

title: Proper Scoring Rules for Missing Value Imputation
text: package also provides density-ratio based DR--Score.
code: sc_dr <- DR_IScore(   X = X,   imputation_func = impute_zero,   m = 3,   n_proj = 10,   n_trees_per_proj = 2,   n_cores = 1 )  sc_dr

title: Proper Scoring Rules for Missing Value Imputation
text: Several methods can compared simultaneously using compare_Iscores().
code: library(mice)  impute_mice_norm <- function(X) {    imp <- mice(     X,     m = 1,     method = "norm",     maxit = 5,     printFlag = FALSE   )    complete(imp) }  impute_mice_rf <- function(X) {    imp <- mice(     X,     m = 1,     method = "rf",     maxit = 5,     printFlag = FALSE   )    complete(imp) }  methods_list <- list(   zero = impute_zero,   norm = impute_mice_norm,   rf = impute_mice_rf )  compare_Iscores(   X = X,   methods_list = methods_list,   score = c("energy_IScore", "DR_IScore"),   N = 10,   m = 3,   silent = TRUE )

title: Proper Scoring Rules for Missing Value Imputation

TRUE, sampling performed missing (NA) observed values. FALSE, sampling performed missing (NA) values.

title: Energy distance — edistance
text: Calculating energy distance/statistic.

title: Energy distance — edistance
code: edistance(X, X_imp, rescale = FALSE)

title: Energy distance — edistance
text: X complete original dataset X_imp imputed dataset rescale logical, indicating whether returned value rescaled. Default FALSE. See "details" section information.

title: Energy distance — edistance
text: function uses eqdist.e function. According implementation, default, function returns energy statistic given $$E(X, Y) = \frac{nm}{n + m} \hat{\varepsilon}{(X, Y)},$$ \(\hat{\varepsilon}{(X, Y)}\) raw energy distance. obtain raw energy distance use rescale = TRUE.

title: Energy distance — edistance
code: X <- matrix(rnorm(100), nrow = 25) X_imp <- matrix(rnorm(100), nrow = 25) edistance(X, X_imp) #> E-statistic  #>    2.930461

what: Calculates Imputation Score for imputation function — energy_IScore
title: Calculates Imputation Score for imputation function — energy_IScore
text: Calculates Imputation Score imputation function

title: Calculates Imputation Score for imputation function — energy_IScore
code: energy_IScore(   X,   imputation_func,   X_imp = NULL,   multiple = TRUE,   N = 50,   max_length = NULL,   skip_if_needed = TRUE,   scale = FALSE,   n_cores = 1,   silent = TRUE )

title: Calculates Imputation Score for imputation function — energy_IScore
text: X data containing missing values denoted NA's. imputation_func function imputes data. X_imp imputed dataset size X. NULL default meaning obtained imputation  X using imputation_func. multiple logical indicating whether provided imputation method multiple imputation approach (.e. generates different values impute call). Default TRUE. Note multiple equals FALSE, N automatically set 1. N numeric value. Number samples imputation distribution H. Default 50. max_length Maximum number variables \(X_j\) consider, can speed code. Default NULL meaning columns taken consideration. skip_if_needed logical, indicating whether observations skipped obtain complete columns scoring. FALSE, NA returned column observed variable training. scale logical value. TRUE, variable scaled score. n_cores number cores parallelization. silent logical indicating whether warnings messages printed.

title: Calculates Imputation Score for imputation function — energy_IScore
text: numerical value denoting weighted Imputation Score obtained provided imputation function table scores weights calculated particular columns.

title: Calculates Imputation Score for imputation function — energy_IScore
text: function relies functions energy_Iscore_num energy_Iscore_cat. Depending presence factor-type data, functions compute score either purely numerical data mixed data types. want compute score numerical data, make sure dataset contain factor-type variables. want compute score categorical data, ensure categorical variables preserved factors. imputation method support categorical variables represented factors, implement wrapper function handles appropriate data type conversions imputation.

title: Calculates Imputation Score for imputation function — energy_IScore
code: set.seed(111) X <- Iscores:::random_mcar_data(100, 4) imputation_func <- Iscores:::exp_imputation energy_IScore(X, imputation_func) #> [1] 0.5785145 #> attr(,"dat") #>    column_id weight     score n_columns_used #> X1         1 0.1875 0.6185805              1 #> X2         2 0.1824 0.5872214              1 #> X4         4 0.1600 0.5669609              1 #> X3         3 0.1476 0.5293823              1  X <-  Iscores:::random_mcar_mixed_data(100, 4, 2) imputation_func <- Iscores:::median_mode_imputation energy_IScore(X, imputation_func) #> Factor variables detected. #>              Calculating the energy-I-Score for mixed data. #> [1] 0.8282569 #> attr(,"dat") #>      column_id weight     score n_columns_used #> col5         5 0.1771 0.9498449              1 #> col1         1 0.1716 0.7088947              1 #> col2         2 0.1600 0.8030265              1 #> col4         4 0.1600 0.7289043              1 #> col3         3 0.1476 0.8242921              1 #> col6         6 0.1204 0.9899495              1

what: energy-I-Score for imputation of mixed data (categorical and numerical) — energy_Iscore_cat
title: energy-I-Score for imputation of mixed data (categorical and numerical) — energy_Iscore_cat
text: energy--Score imputation mixed data (categorical numerical)

title: energy-I-Score for imputation of mixed data (categorical and numerical) — energy_Iscore_cat
code: energy_Iscore_cat(   X,   imputation_func,   X_imp = imputation_func(X),   multiple = TRUE,   N = 50,   max_length = NULL,   skip_if_needed = TRUE,   scale = FALSE,   n_cores = 1,   silent = TRUE )

title: energy-I-Score for imputation of mixed data (categorical and numerical) — energy_Iscore_cat
text: X data containing missing values denoted NA's. imputation_func function imputes data. X_imp imputed dataset size X. NULL default meaning obtained imputation  X using imputation_func. multiple logical indicating whether provided imputation method multiple imputation approach (.e. generates different values impute call). Default TRUE. Note multiple equals FALSE, N automatically set 1. N numeric value. Number samples imputation distribution H. Default 50. max_length Maximum number variables \(X_j\) consider, can speed code. Default NULL meaning columns taken consideration. skip_if_needed logical, indicating whether observations skipped obtain complete columns scoring. FALSE, NA returned column observed variable training. scale logical value. TRUE, variable scaled score. n_cores number cores parallelization. silent logical indicating whether warnings messages printed.

title: energy-I-Score for imputation of mixed data (categorical and numerical) — energy_Iscore_cat
text: numerical value denoting weighted Imputation Score obtained provided imputation function table scores weights calculated particular columns.

title: energy-I-Score for imputation of mixed data (categorical and numerical) — energy_Iscore_cat
text: categorical variables stored factors. need additional conversion data (example one-hot encoding) imputation, please, implement everything within imputation_func parameter. can use miceDRF:::onehot_to_factor miceDRF:::factor_to_onehot functions.

title: energy-I-Score for imputation of mixed data (categorical and numerical) — energy_Iscore_cat
text: method described detail : Näf, J., Grzesiak, K., Scornet, E. (2025a). rank imputation methods? arXiv preprint arXiv:2507.11297 (https://doi.org/10.48550/arXiv.2507.11297).

title: energy-I-Score for imputation of mixed data (categorical and numerical) — energy_Iscore_cat
code: set.seed(123)  X <- Iscores:::random_mcar_mixed_data(n = 100, p = 3, n_fac = 1, ratio = 0.2)  imp_fun <- Iscores:::median_mode_imputation  sc <- Iscores::energy_Iscore_cat(X = X, imputation_func = imp_fun, N = 5) #> Error: 'energy_Iscore_cat' is not an exported object from 'namespace:Iscores'  sc #> Error: object 'sc' not found

title: Extract and group missing-data patterns — get_pattern_data
text: list three elements: patterns matrix unique missingness patterns. groups list integer vectors giving row indices pattern. average_diff logical indicating whether singleton patterns merged.

title: Extract and group missing-data patterns — get_pattern_data
text: Missingness patterns represented logical matrix obtained .na(X). rows containing least one missing value used define unique patterns. one pattern represented single observation, singleton patterns merged using merge_singleton_patterns().
